chunks.title: Salary Expectations on MENA CVs
chunks.concrete_rule: Do NOT default to including a salary expectation on the CV. Only surface a salary line when (a) the candidate has explicitly entered an expected number in their profile AND (b) the target JD explicitly asks for "expected salary" or the target market is Egyptian local-employer (non-multinational). When salary is included, format it as **"Expected Salary: {amount} {currency} / month, net"** for Egypt and Maghreb, and **"Expected Package: {amount} {currency} / month (base + standard allowances)"** for the Gulf. Place the line in the Personal Information block, not in a free-text cover-note paragraph. For US, UK, EU, or multinational targets, omit the field entirely even if the candidate provided it.
chunks.body:
# Salary Expectations on MENA CVs

Stating a salary expectation on the CV — or in the cover note — is materially more common in MENA than in the US or UK, where the dominant advice is to defer the conversation until offer stage. There is no public source mandating a salary line on a MENA CV, but it appears regularly in three contexts:

1. **Egyptian private-sector applications via Wuzzuf or direct email.** Many JDs explicitly ask "Expected Salary (EGP, monthly net)" as an application question. Candidates who skip the field are often filtered out automatically.
2. **Gulf roles for expatriate candidates.** Because total compensation in the Gulf is built from a base salary plus housing allowance, transport allowance, schooling allowance, annual flight, and end-of-service gratuity, employers want a single "all-in expected package" number to compare candidates against budget bands.
3. **Egyptian fresh-grad and intern postings.** Because pay bands are wide and informal, recruiters often ask for the candidate's number first to avoid wasting interview cycles on a misaligned candidate.

Currency conventions:

- **Egypt:** EGP, monthly, usually quoted **net** (after tax and social-insurance deduction).
- **Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman:** SAR / AED / QAR / KWD / BHD / OMR, monthly base + benefits broken out separately. Income tax does not apply on personal salary in most Gulf jurisdictions, so "net" and "gross" overlap.
- **Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria:** MAD / TND / DZD, monthly net.

Counter-pattern: multinational subsidiaries (Microsoft, P&G, Unilever, Vodafone) operating in MENA generally follow their global policy and do NOT want a salary expectation on the CV — it is collected via a separate application form field or HR phone screen.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

Do NOT default to including a salary expectation on the CV. Only surface a salary line when (a) the candidate has explicitly entered an expected number in their profile AND (b) the target JD explicitly asks for "expected salary" or the target market is Egyptian local-employer (non-multinational). When salary is included, format it as **"Expected Salary: {amount} {currency} / month, net"** for Egypt and Maghreb, and **"Expected Package: {amount} {currency} / month (base + standard allowances)"** for the Gulf. Place the line in the Personal Information block, not in a free-text cover-note paragraph. For US, UK, EU, or multinational targets, omit the field entirely even if the candidate provided it.

## What public sources document

**Salary on the Wuzzuf structured profile is a documented ranking input.** Wuzzuf's own help center (via search excerpt, 2026-05-12) states that "adding a net salary will improve your job matching recommendation." This confirms the structured-profile salary field is not purely informational; it influences which jobs the platform surfaces and how the candidate is ranked against employer searches.

**2025–2026 Egypt CS / software-engineer salary data (monthly figures unless noted):**

- Wuzzuf's own posting for "Computer Science & Computer Engineering Fresh Graduates" (Heliopolis, Cairo) advertises **up to EGP 8,000/month** starting (Wuzzuf job posting, accessed 2026-05-12 via search excerpt).
- Glassdoor (2026): Junior Software Engineer in Cairo typical pay range **EGP 7,583 – EGP 17,063** annually (note: this annualised range is suspiciously low and likely a Glassdoor unit-conversion artefact; Glassdoor data for Egypt is patchy and should be treated as a directional lower bound).
- Whatisthesalary.com (2026): Fresh-graduate software engineer salary in Egypt **EGP 10,000 – 25,000 / month**; junior roles average **EGP 15,000 – 25,000 / month**.
- PayScale (2026): Junior Software Engineer in Egypt — typical range EGP 6,016 – 15,840 annually (same likely-annualised artefact as above).

**Wuzzuf's published salary analytics for Egypt** (`wuzzuf.net/careers/`, accessed 2026-05-12 via search excerpts):
- "Arabic speakers receive approximately 5,500 EGP on average for entry-level positions, while English speakers collect around 13,000 EGP" — i.e., language adds a ~2.4× multiplier on entry-level pay.
- "French speakers in Egypt can collect 27,000 EGP or beyond" in industries where Arabic speakers earn 5,500 EGP.
- Customer-service median (2025): **EGP 5,550** Arabic-only, **EGP 13,500** with English fluency.
- Entry-level call-center: **EGP 7,000 – 9,000 / month**.

**Net-vs-gross convention** is documented in Wuzzuf's own tax-calculator content: full-time employees deduct 11% social insurance, subtract the EGP 20,000 annual exemption, and apply progressive tax rates; freelancers pay a flat 10% income tax. This confirms the file's claim that Egyptian salaries are quoted **net** on Wuzzuf.

I could not find a public source documenting verbatim Egyptian-recruiter phrasing ("ابعتلي رقم"), cultural penalties for over-quoting, or USD-pegging behaviour after the 2022–2024 EGP devaluation.

## Still needed from the author

- The specific 2026 salary bands KSIU CS classmates have actually received (intern / junior / multinational / remote-foreign-payer).
- Whether Wuzzuf's "Expected Salary" application-form field is gate-keeping in practice — does leaving it blank reduce callbacks?
- Whether Cairo recruiters say "ابعتلي رقم" verbatim and how candidates handle it.
- Whether the cultural penalty for over-quoting in Egypt is genuinely heavier than for under-quoting in the author's circle.
- Post-2022 EGP-devaluation behaviour: do mid-career candidates now quote in USD, "negotiable", or stick to fixed EGP?
- Whether multinationals in Egypt (Vodafone Egypt, Orange Egypt, Etisalat-by-e&, Microsoft, IBM, Dell) ask the salary question on the CV, the application form, or in the HR phone screen.

---

chunks.title: Language Fields — Arabic, English, French Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: Always include a Languages section on MENA CVs (it is rarely omitted). Default to the descriptive scale (Native / Fluent / Advanced / Intermediate / Basic) for local-employer targets and the CEFR scale (A1–C2) for multinational and EU-headquartered targets. Order languages by regional weight: for Mashreq targets, Arabic → English → others; for Maghreb targets, Arabic → French → English → others. Always include the candidate's native language explicitly with the label "Native" — do not assume Arabic is implied. If the candidate speaks Berber/Tamazight, include it (it is constitutionally official in Morocco and Algeria).
chunks.body:
# Language Fields — Arabic, English, French Conventions

The Languages section is more load-bearing on a MENA CV than on a US one, because the regional labour market is genuinely multilingual and the candidate's specific language mix changes which roles they qualify for.

**Mashreq (Egypt, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, Syria, Iraq, Palestine, Yemen):**
- Arabic is the official language. Egypt's official language is Modern Standard Arabic ("Arabic" with "Modern Standard Arabic" as the official register and "Egyptian Arabic" as the national variety).
- English is the de facto language of business in the Gulf — Wikipedia notes for the UAE: "Arabic is the official language while English remains the most spoken language and the language of business."
- The candidate is expected to declare proficiency in **Arabic AND English** at minimum.

**Maghreb (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia):**
- Arabic and Berber are the official languages. In Morocco "the official languages of Morocco are Modern Standard Arabic and Standard Moroccan Amazigh." In Algeria "the official languages of Algeria are Arabic and Standard Algerian Amazigh."
- French is the practical language of business and higher education. Wikipedia notes for Morocco: "French... is often used for business, diplomacy, and government; and serves as a lingua franca" and "serves as Morocco's primary language of commerce and economics, culture, sciences and medicine." For Algeria: "French continues to be the dominant language in business and professional circles" and "All scientific and business university courses are still taught in French."
- The candidate is expected to declare **Arabic, French, and English** — usually in that order of regional weight, though English is rising fast for tech roles.

Proficiency scales:

- The European-origin **CEFR scale** (A1–C2 — Wikipedia: "C2 (Proficient User): Can 'understand with ease virtually everything heard or read'") is used by some MENA candidates with European-style education (GUC, AUC, French-system schools). It carries weight at multinationals.
- The **descriptive scale** (Native / Fluent / Advanced / Intermediate / Basic) is far more common on local-employer CVs and on Wuzzuf / Bayt structured profiles.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

Always include a Languages section on MENA CVs (it is rarely omitted). Default to the descriptive scale (Native / Fluent / Advanced / Intermediate / Basic) for local-employer targets and the CEFR scale (A1–C2) for multinational and EU-headquartered targets. Order languages by regional weight: for Mashreq targets, Arabic → English → others; for Maghreb targets, Arabic → French → English → others. Always include the candidate's native language explicitly with the label "Native" — do not assume Arabic is implied. If the candidate speaks Berber/Tamazight, include it (it is constitutionally official in Morocco and Algeria).

## What public sources document

**Language directly affects pay in Egypt** — confirmed by Wuzzuf's own salary analytics (`wuzzuf.net/careers/how-language-skills-can-increase-your-salary/` and `how-language-decides-customer-service-salary/`, accessed 2026-05-12 via search excerpts): Arabic-only entry-level pay averages ~EGP 5,500/month, English-fluent pay averages ~EGP 13,000/month (a ~2.4× multiplier on identical work), and French-fluent roles in the same industries can reach EGP 27,000/month. The Languages section is therefore not cosmetic on an Egyptian CV — it is a salary-bracket determinant.

**Maghreb language expectations** are confirmed across multiple sources (accessed 2026-05-12 via search excerpts):
- Morocco: French "dominates corporate roles such as mining and banking"; "in most government offices, banks, and many other contexts, you are expected to know either French or Arabic" (Crossroads Cultural Exchange, accessed 2026-05-12). CV-advice sources (resumeflex.com, resume-example.com, accessed 2026-05-12) recommend the CV itself be in French or Arabic, with English acceptable only at multinationals and in IT/tourism.
- Formal certifications "**DELF (French), IELTS (English), or DELE (Spanish) add credibility**" on Maghreb CVs (resume-example.com, accessed 2026-05-12).

The CEFR scale (A1–C2) is the standard European framework used by multinationals and EU-headquartered firms (Wikipedia, *Common European Framework of Reference for Languages*, accessed 2026-05-12 — already cited in this file's headline section).

I could not directly verify Wuzzuf's structured language-proficiency dropdown values (Native / Fluent / Good / Fair / Basic) because the platform's profile-builder UI is gated behind a login and the help-center articles redirect to blocked URLs. The Bayt blog excerpt mentions language fields as part of profile completion but does not specify the dropdown values.

## Still needed from the author

- The literal Wuzzuf language-proficiency dropdown values in 2026.
- KSIU and Cairo-University CS grad self-reporting patterns — do they overwhelmingly write "Fluent" English when the honest level is "Good" or "Advanced"?
- Whether B2+ CEFR gating is in fact concentrated at German-system firms (Siemens, Valeo, Bosch), GUC-affiliated employers, UN agencies, and the EBRD.
- Whether French banks in Casablanca, Algiers, and Tunis reject English-only CVs.
- Recruiter mid-call language-switch verification — does this actually happen in practice, and at what employer category?
- How TOEFL / IELTS scores are typically presented alongside CEFR claims on Egyptian CVs.

---

chunks.title: MENA University Recognition on CVs
chunks.concrete_rule: When the candidate's degree is from a MENA university and the target job is **outside MENA**, append the international accreditation in parentheses on the Education line — for example: "B.Sc. Computer Engineering, KFUPM (ABET-accredited), 2020–2024" or "B.A. Economics, AUC (Middle States accredited), 2020–2024". When the target is **inside MENA**, omit the accreditation note (it is assumed locally and adds clutter). Never claim accreditation that is not supported by the institution's public record. For lesser-known institutions (KSIU and similar), default to including the city ("KSIU, Cairo, Egypt") to provide geographic context for the reviewer.
chunks.body:
# MENA University Recognition on CVs

How a MENA university reads on a CV depends heavily on the audience. The same degree carries different signal weight inside MENA versus at a US/UK employer that has never heard of the institution. Public ranking and accreditation data anchor the comparison.

**Egypt:**
- **Cairo University** — Founded "21 December 1908." "Ranked 1st in Egypt" by ARWU 2020, CWUR 2020-21, USNWR 2022. QS World 2026: =347. Strongly recognised inside MENA; mid-recognition outside (often needs degree-equivalency certification for US grad-school applications).
- **The American University in Cairo (AUC)** — Founded 1919. "Accredited by the Middle States Commission on Higher Education in the United States" and "incorporated by the State of Delaware." Ranked 411th worldwide and 9th in the Arab Region (QS 2021). High recognition outside MENA because of US accreditation.
- **German University in Cairo (GUC)** — Founded 2002. "The language of instruction is English." Programs "accredited in Egypt and Germany (by ACQUIN)." Recognised by the German Federal Foreign Office, which "lists GUC first among the ten educational institutions currently being promoted." Strong signal in DE/EU markets.

**Saudi Arabia:**
- **King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals (KFUPM)** — Founded 1963 (College), university status 1975. ABET-accredited engineering programs (rated as "Substantial-Equivalency" to US programs). AACSB-accredited business programs (member since 1975). "Ranks 5th and 7th globally in petroleum engineering & mining engineering" (QS subjects). 67th globally in QS World 2026.
- **King Saud University** — Founded 1957 by Royal Decree No. 17, "the first university in Saudi Arabia." QS World 143 (2026). AACSB-accredited business school.
- **King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)** — "Founded in 2009." "Provides research and graduate training programs in English as the official language of instruction." US News Global 100 (2024-25). Strong international research profile.

The accreditation signal (ABET, AACSB, ACQUIN, Middle States) is the single most legible cue to a non-MENA hiring manager. A CV that names the accrediting body next to the institution is materially more legible to US/EU/UK reviewers than one that names only the degree.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

When the candidate's degree is from a MENA university and the target job is **outside MENA**, append the international accreditation in parentheses on the Education line — for example: "B.Sc. Computer Engineering, KFUPM (ABET-accredited), 2020–2024" or "B.A. Economics, AUC (Middle States accredited), 2020–2024". When the target is **inside MENA**, omit the accreditation note (it is assumed locally and adds clutter). Never claim accreditation that is not supported by the institution's public record. For lesser-known institutions (KSIU and similar), default to including the city ("KSIU, Cairo, Egypt") to provide geographic context for the reviewer.

## Correction to earlier drafts

A factual error in an earlier draft of this file is corrected here. **KSIU stands for King Salman International University**, not "Kafr El-Sheikh International University". KSIU is a Sinai-based "national (Ahleya), non-profit Egyptian university" with three campuses at Ras Sedr, El Tor, and Sharm El Sheikh, established in August 2020 by presidential decision and named after King Salman of Saudi Arabia (Wikipedia, *King Salman International University*; ksiu.edu.eg; sis.gov.eg accreditation list; all accessed 2026-05-12). **Kafr El-Sheikh University** (KFS) is a separate institution — a governmental university founded in 2006 in the Nile Delta city of Kafr El-Sheikh, recognised by Egypt's Ministry of Higher Education (topuniversities.com KFS page, accessed 2026-05-12). The two should not be conflated. Suggested-prompt wording in the header that says "How do KSIU grads actually present... 'Kafr El-Sheikh'?" reflects the same earlier confusion.

## What public sources document

**KSIU (King Salman International University)** is on Egypt's official State Information Service list of accredited foreign and government universities. KSIU offers 33 programs across 13 faculties, including Computer Science (at the El Tor campus). The Computer Science faculty is documented as "pursuing local and international accreditation" rather than holding it (KSIU and unirank pages, accessed 2026-05-12). As a 2020-founded institution, KSIU does not yet have programmatic accreditation from ABET, AACSB, ACQUIN, or Middle States.

**Cairo University** is recognised by Egypt's Ministry of Higher Education and ranks 1st in Egypt by ARWU, CWUR, and USNWR (already documented in the headline section). The Cairo-University-vs-AUC distinction is important: ABET-accredited Computer Science and Engineering programs in Egypt are concentrated at **AUC** (per AUC's own accreditation page, accessed 2026-05-12 — "the undergraduate computer science program is accredited by the Computing Accreditation Commission of ABET", and "for the fourth consecutive time, AUC's computer science and engineering (CSE) program was reaccredited by ABET"). I was unable to confirm whether Cairo University's national Faculty of Engineering also holds ABET accreditation for its CS program; ABET's accredited-programs database (accreditation.org, accessed 2026-05-12) is the authoritative check the author should run before claiming this on a CV.

**KFUPM** holds ABET accreditation at every degree level and is the only Saudi university to do so. All KFUPM engineering and computing programs are ABET-accredited on a 6-year revision cycle (KFUPM official ABET pages, accessed 2026-05-12). KFUPM is also "the first Saudi university that acquired accreditation for both its programs and the institution as a whole." This anchors the file's claim that KFUPM is the regional gold-standard accreditation signal.

**AUC's international footing** is documented: "Out of 523 institutions worldwide that are accredited by MSCHE, only 17 — including AUC — are outside the United States" (aucegypt.edu accreditation page, accessed 2026-05-12). AUC's Business School holds AACSB accreditation. AUC is also "the first University in Egypt to be accredited at the institutional level by NAQAAE" (Egypt's National Authority for Quality Assurance and Accreditation of Education).

## Still needed from the author

- How KSIU grads actually present "King Salman International University" to non-Egyptian recruiters — full name, "KSIU", with campus location, with "Sinai, Egypt" tag?
- The actual recruiter-weighted Egyptian CS pecking order in the author's observation (AUC, GUC, Cairo, Ain Shams, Alexandria, Mansoura, private universities, KSIU).
- Whether AUC and GUC grads in fact receive preferential Gulf-employer treatment in the author's circle.
- Cairo-University CS / Computer Engineering's actual ABET status (the author should check ABET's database directly).
- Whether KSIU's accreditation track has moved since the 2020 founding.
- WES / ECE evaluation experience for Egyptian degrees — cost, turnaround, registrar requirements — from anyone the author knows who has gone through it.
- Which post-2010 Egyptian for-profit campuses recruiters discount on sight (the author should name them only if confident, since this is reputation-sensitive).

---

chunks.title: MENA Working Week and Calendar Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: Always use Gregorian dates on the CV with month names spelled out (e.g., "Jan 2023 – Present", "Sep 2020 – Jun 2024"). Never use Hijri dates on a CV, even for Saudi-targeted CVs. Never use purely numeric date formats (01/02/2024 is ambiguous between MENA DD/MM and US MM/DD). When the candidate provides an "Available from" date, assume Gregorian and present it in the same "Mon YYYY" format. Do not infer or display workweek expectations on the CV itself; surface them only in cover-letter context if the JD explicitly asks about availability.
chunks.body:
# MENA Working Week and Calendar Conventions

The MENA region does not share a single workweek convention, which has direct implications for date formats on CVs, availability statements, and notice-period clauses.

**Workweek by country (per Wikipedia's Workweek and weekend article):**

- **Sunday–Thursday workweek (Friday–Saturday weekend):** Egypt, Iraq, Libya, Sudan, Syria, Yemen, Algeria, Bahrain. Egypt: "40 hours per week, 8 hours daily."
- **Sunday–Thursday workweek (Friday–Saturday weekend, post-2013 reform):** Saudi Arabia ("Saudi Arabia switches start of weekend from Thursday to Friday" in 2013). Kuwait (since 2007), Qatar, Oman (changed 2013), Jordan (changed in the "Week of January 8, 2000"), Palestine.
- **Monday–Friday workweek (Saturday–Sunday weekend):** UAE (since 2022 — "Friday is half working day from 8 am until noon"), Lebanon, Morocco ("the standard workweek in Morocco is from Monday to Friday"), Tunisia ("Monday to Friday; 8 hours per day, 40 hours in total per week").

**Ramadan adjustments** apply across the region: Saudi Arabia reduces to "30-36 hours" weekly; Kuwait and Qatar to "25 hours"; Oman to "30 hours."

**Calendar conventions:**

- **Gregorian calendar** is the default for all CV dates region-wide.
- **Hijri (Islamic) calendar** appears on official Saudi documents but is not used on CVs. Wikipedia notes: "On 14 February 2016, Saudi Arabia adopted the Gregorian calendar for payment of the monthly salaries of government employees (as a cost cutting measure), while retaining the Islamic calendar for religious purposes."
- **Dual dating** (Gregorian + Hijri) appears on government identity documents but is unnecessary on a CV. Date formats vary: DD/MM/YYYY is dominant region-wide, MM/DD/YYYY is sometimes seen on US-template CVs adopted in Egypt.

Why this matters on a CV:

- "Available immediately" reads differently in a market with a Sun-Thu week vs. a Mon-Fri week — start dates need to align.
- A Saudi candidate with a 30-day notice period and a Mon-Fri-week target employer may need to clarify weekend conventions.
- Date ranges on the CV ("Jan 2023 – Present") should always be Gregorian and unambiguous (use month names, not numerals, to avoid DD/MM vs MM/DD ambiguity for cross-region applications).

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

Always use Gregorian dates on the CV with month names spelled out (e.g., "Jan 2023 – Present", "Sep 2020 – Jun 2024"). Never use Hijri dates on a CV, even for Saudi-targeted CVs. Never use purely numeric date formats (01/02/2024 is ambiguous between MENA DD/MM and US MM/DD). When the candidate provides an "Available from" date, assume Gregorian and present it in the same "Mon YYYY" format. Do not infer or display workweek expectations on the CV itself; surface them only in cover-letter context if the JD explicitly asks about availability.

## What public sources document

**Egypt's workweek is documented across multiple labor-law and HR sources** (accessed 2026-05-12):
- Sunday–Thursday workweek with Friday–Saturday weekend is standard (playroll.com Egypt working hours; EgyptToday labor-law report; Multiplier Egypt employment-laws guide).
- Statutory ceiling under Egyptian labor law (Article 117): "8 hours per day or 48 hours per week" excluding meals and rests (riad-riad.com overview).
- Article 120 mandates "a continuous weekly rest period of no less than 24 hours with full pay" after at most 6 consecutive working days.
- Many private-sector offices voluntarily adopt **40 hours / 5 days** within the 48-hour statutory ceiling (playroll.com; rivermate.com).
- Egypt is described as having a "usual work week" of "six consecutive days" in some sources (justlanded.com), confirming the six-day-week pattern in sectors like retail and logistics survives alongside the office five-day norm.
- Some Egyptian private companies implement a half-day Thursday schedule (multiple HR sources, accessed 2026-05-12).

**Wuzzuf's own published labor-law overview** (`wuzzuf.net/careers/working-hours-in-the-new-egyptian-labor-law-2/`, accessed 2026-05-12 — direct fetch HTTP 403, found via search result) confirms Wuzzuf treats these statutory rules as the candidate-facing reference.

**UAE's 2022 workweek change** is documented in detail (Al Jazeera, CNBC, Addleshaw Goddard, Lockton, Lexology; accessed 2026-05-12). The shift was announced in December 2021 by state news agency WAM and took effect 1 January 2022 for federal government entities. Schedule: Monday–Thursday 07:30–15:30 with Friday a half-day (07:30–12:00 in observance of Jumu'ah), and Saturday–Sunday weekend. The private sector was not legally bound but "most private sector companies were anticipated to align" with the federal change.

**Saudi Arabia's calendar move** is confirmed by Arab News and the Wikipedia Islamic Calendar article (accessed 2026-05-12): on 14 February 2016 the Saudi Cabinet adopted the Gregorian calendar "for payment of the monthly salaries of government employees (as a cost cutting measure), while retaining the Islamic calendar for religious purposes." The Umm al-Qura (Saudi civil Hijri) calendar continues to be used for legal documents and administrative purposes. CV dates region-wide are Gregorian.

**Visa Status with NOC convention** for Gulf CVs is documented (accessed 2026-05-12 via search excerpts) — "Transferable Iqama – No NOC required" and "Employment Visa – Transferable" are canonical phrases that recruiters search for. This connects to file 004's nationality/visa-status content.

I could not find a public source confirming Wuzzuf's exact date-format defaults (Mon YYYY vs numeric), nor whether Egyptian CVs ever carry a Ramadan-hours line, nor the typical "Notice Period" values used on Egyptian mid-career CVs.

## Still needed from the author

- The literal date-format Wuzzuf displays in profile work-experience entries (Mon YYYY in English, يناير YYYY in Arabic, or numeric).
- Whether the Egyptian 6-day-week sectors (retail, logistics, manufacturing, family firms) ever ask candidates to signal flexibility on the CV.
- Hijri-date requests in private-sector Saudi applications (the author's experience suggests rarely or never; this should be confirmed).
- Standard Notice-Period values on Egyptian mid-career CVs and where the line is placed.
- Whether the 2022 UAE Mon–Fri switch is now invisible in recruiter conversations or still mentioned.

---
sources:
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Workweek_and_weekend  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.playroll.com/working-hours/egypt  (accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)
  - https://www.egypttoday.com/Article/1/145902/Report-on-Labor-Law-and-working-hour-regulations-in-Egyptian  (accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)
  - https://www.usemultiplier.com/egypt/employment-laws  (accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)
  - https://riad-riad.com/overview-of-the-egyptian-labor-law/  (accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)
  - https://wuzzuf.net/careers/working-hours-in-the-new-egyptian-labor-law-2/  (accessed 2026-05-12; direct fetch HTTP 403, via search excerpt)
  - https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2021/12/7/uae-announces-changes-to-workweek-for-employees-of-govt-sector  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.cnbc.com/2022/03/14/uae-is-warming-to-its-new-monday-to-friday-working-week.html  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.addleshawgoddard.com/en/insights/insights-briefings/2021/employment/new-working-week-in-the-uae/  (accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)
  - https://www.arabnews.com/node/2401501/saudi-arabia  (Saudi Cabinet Gregorian-calendar adoption; accessed 2026-05-12; via search excerpt)

chunks.title: MENA AI/ML Hiring Conventions — Vision 2030, Arabic-NLP, Trainee Tracks
chunks.concrete_rule: For ml_engineer resumes targeting MENA markets, align vocabulary with the SPECIFIC regional strategy of the target country: Saudi Vision 2030 / SDAIA / NEOM AI for Saudi-targeted CVs only; National AI Strategy 2031 / MBZUAI / G42 / Inception for UAE-targeted CVs only; Digital Egypt 2030 / National Council for AI / DEPI / MCIT programs for Egyptian targets only. Do not surface Saudi vocabulary on Egyptian applications or vice versa — cross-pollination signals MENA-tourist framing, not market awareness. Surface Arabic-NLP capability when present (AraBERT, CAMeL Tools, Jais, Falcon-Arabic, AraT5) — the single strongest MENA-specific differentiator that generic "AI engineer" framing misses. For Egyptian fresh-graduates with DEPI / MCIT-program backgrounds, preserve "AI Trainee" / "Data Science Trainee" titles verbatim — auto-promoting to "AI Engineer" mis-aligns the seniority band.
chunks.body:
# MENA AI/ML Hiring Conventions — Vision 2030, Arabic-NLP, Trainee Tracks

The MENA AI/ML hiring market sits on top of explicit national strategies (Saudi Vision 2030 with SDAIA; Egypt Vision 2030 with the National Council for AI; UAE national AI strategy with G42 and MBZUAI) that produce specific recruiting-signal vocabulary. A generic "AI Engineer" or "ML Engineer" framing translates the candidate's work onto a global resume template — useful for FAANG-targeted applications but underweighted for regional hiring where alignment with Vision-strategy initiatives, regional AI institutions, and Arabic-language capability are credibility signals. This chunk surfaces the vocabulary; the candidate's source CV provides the substance.

**The three national-strategy anchors:**

1. **Saudi Arabia — Vision 2030 / SDAIA / NEOM AI / Aramco D&AI.** Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 (announced 2016, ongoing through 2030) names AI as a pillar of economic diversification. The Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA, established 2019) is the central institution; NEOM's AI-first design and Aramco's Digital & AI Center are the visible execution arms. CVs targeting Riyadh / Jeddah / Dammam-based AI roles benefit from explicit Vision 2030 vocabulary in the Summary if the candidate's work touches the pillar themes (national-AI capability, Arabic-language AI, energy-sector AI, smart-city AI).
2. **UAE — National AI Strategy 2031 / MBZUAI / G42 / Inception.** UAE was the first country to appoint a Minister of State for AI (2017). MBZUAI (Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence, founded 2019 in Abu Dhabi) is the dedicated graduate university; G42 (incorporated 2018) and its operating units (Inception, Core42, Khazna) drive the commercial AI execution; Falcon LLM is the visible Arabic-LLM output. CVs targeting Dubai / Abu Dhabi roles benefit from explicit awareness of these institutions — Inception alone has hired hundreds of ML engineers since 2022.
3. **Egypt — Digital Egypt 2030 / National Council for AI / DEPI / MCIT programs.** Egypt's National Council for AI (established 2019 under MCIT) coordinates national AI strategy. The Digital Egypt Pioneers Initiative (DEPI, launched 2024 by MCIT) is the visible workforce-building program training 100,000+ youth in digital skills including AI/ML. Egyptian fresh-graduate AI candidates very often hold "AI Trainee" or "Data Science Trainee" titles reflecting DEPI / MCIT program structure — these are legitimate role labels, not under-titling.

**Arabic-NLP as a MENA differentiator.**

Arabic-language NLP is the single strongest MENA-specific technical signal an ML engineer can surface. The major resources are widely cited:

- **AraBERT** (Antoun et al. 2020, arXiv:2003.00104) — the first BERT-style model pretrained on Arabic; AUB / CAMeL Lab. Now superseded by many variants but remains the canonical Arabic-BERT reference.
- **CAMeL Tools** (NYU Abu Dhabi) — open-source toolkit for Arabic NLP preprocessing (morphological analysis, dialect ID, MSA / dialect normalization).
- **Jais** (Inception / G42 / MBZUAI, arXiv:2308.16149) — 13B / 30B Arabic-English bilingual LLM. The flagship MENA-region open-source LLM as of 2024-2026.
- **Falcon-Arabic** (Technology Innovation Institute / G42) — Arabic adaptations of the Falcon family.
- **AraT5, AraGPT2, MARBERT, ARBERT** — domain-specific Arabic models (T5-style, GPT-style, multi-dialect).

A CV that includes Arabic-NLP project work (using AraBERT, CAMeL Tools, Jais, or any Arabic-adapted model) signals capability that no generic "AI engineer" framing captures. For MENA hiring managers, this is often more compelling than a generic Llama-fine-tuning bullet.

**Trainee vs. Junior title convention (Egyptian specifics).**

Egyptian AI/ML candidates entering the workforce through DEPI, MCIT-sponsored bootcamps, ITIDA programs, or industry-sponsored tracks (Banque Misr Future Bank Academy, Vodafone Egypt Discover, etc.) very often hold:

- "AI Trainee" / "Data Science Trainee" / "Machine Learning Trainee" — 3-12 month structured programs, often paid stipend, with a defined curriculum + mentorship + capstone project. **These are credible role labels**, not under-titling. The Trainee designation reflects the program structure, not the candidate's competence.
- "AI Engineer Apprentice" / "ML Engineer Trainee" — longer programs (12-24 months) with a defined promotion path to "AI Engineer" or "Junior ML Engineer" on completion.

Auto-promoting a Trainee title to "AI Engineer" mis-aligns the seniority band (per `seniority_norms/005_ml_junior` and `seniority_norms/006_ml_mid`) and can cause the resume to apply to roles the candidate isn't yet qualified for. Preserve the source-CV title verbatim.

**BAD -> GOOD transformations:**

- BAD (generic, MENA-context absent): "Built an LLM-powered chatbot for customer support."
- GOOD (MENA-aware): "Built an Arabic-English bilingual support chatbot using Jais-13b-chat (G42/Inception, arXiv:2308.16149) over a 12K-document Arabic + English KB (CAMeL Tools for Arabic normalization, all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for retrieval); deployed in pilot with 4 Saudi banking customer-support teams; auto-resolution rate 34% on Arabic queries (vs. 0% pre-deploy with English-only model)."

- BAD (under-titled): "AI Engineer at Vodafone Egypt Discover Track (2024-2025)."
- GOOD (title-accurate, structure-explicit): "AI Engineer Trainee, Vodafone Egypt Discover Track (Sep 2024 - present); 12-month structured AI/ML program (cohort: 24 trainees); capstone: <named project> on <named dataset>; mentor: <Senior Title>."

- BAD (institution-vocabulary absent): "Worked on AI projects relevant to government digital transformation."
- GOOD (Vision-2030-aligned): "Contributed to a Saudi Vision-2030-aligned smart-city pilot at the Riyadh Municipality (NEOM-affiliated subcontract); built the bilingual-text classifier component (Arabic + English; AraBERT-large; F1 0.86 on the 4-class held-out test set); deployed as a Microsoft-Azure-hosted endpoint serving the Madinati app."

**Anti-patterns specific to MENA AI/ML CVs:**

- **MENA-tourist framing.** A Cairo-targeted CV that name-drops Saudi Vision 2030 and SDAIA, or a Riyadh-targeted CV that references DEPI and Egypt's National Council for AI, reads as someone who pattern-matched "MENA" without understanding the specific market. Recruiters in each capital are sensitive to country-specific vocabulary; cross-pollination signals tourism, not market awareness. Surface only the target country's vocabulary; if the candidate has genuinely worked across multiple MENA markets, surface each market's vocabulary only in the bullets describing work for that market.
- Generic "AI Engineer" framing on a Saudi or UAE CV that ignores Vision 2030 / SDAIA / MBZUAI / G42 vocabulary. The roles exist within a national-strategy context; the CV should signal awareness.
- Listing only English-language models and frameworks when the candidate has Arabic-NLP capability. For MENA hiring this is leaving the strongest signal on the table.
- Translating "AI Trainee" to "AI Engineer" on the assumption that Trainee is under-titling. Trainee programs are legitimate first roles in the MENA market; the structure is the credential.
- Photo / nationality / language-fields absent on a MENA-targeted AI CV. The standard MENA-CV conventions (see `mena_context/002_personal_info_fields`, `mena_context/003_photo_norms`, `mena_context/004_religion_nationality_fields`, `mena_context/008_language_fields_arabic_english_french`) still apply to AI/ML CVs; the AI specialization adds to those conventions, doesn't replace them.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

For ml_engineer resumes targeting MENA markets, align vocabulary with the SPECIFIC regional strategy of the target country: Saudi Vision 2030 / SDAIA / NEOM AI for Saudi-targeted CVs only; National AI Strategy 2031 / MBZUAI / G42 / Inception for UAE-targeted CVs only; Digital Egypt 2030 / National Council for AI / DEPI / MCIT programs for Egyptian targets only. Do not surface Saudi vocabulary on Egyptian applications or vice versa — cross-pollination signals MENA-tourist framing, not market awareness. Surface Arabic-NLP capability when present (AraBERT, CAMeL Tools, Jais, Falcon-Arabic, AraT5) — the single strongest MENA-specific differentiator that generic "AI engineer" framing misses. For Egyptian fresh-graduates with DEPI / MCIT-program backgrounds, preserve "AI Trainee" / "Data Science Trainee" titles verbatim — auto-promoting to "AI Engineer" mis-aligns the seniority band.

---
sources:
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.00104  (Antoun et al., "AraBERT: Transformer-based Model for Arabic Language Understanding", 2020, accessed 2026-05-16)
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.16149  (Sengupta et al., "Jais and Jais-chat: Arabic-Centric Foundation and Instruction-Tuned Open Generative Large Language Models", 2023 — G42 / Inception / MBZUAI, accessed 2026-05-16)
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saudi_Vision_2030  (accessed 2026-05-16)
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohamed_bin_Zayed_University_of_Artificial_Intelligence  (accessed 2026-05-16)
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G42_(company)  (accessed 2026-05-16)

chunks.title: Intern / Student Resume Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: For intern / student resumes, generate one-page output with Education at the top, followed by Skills, then Projects (3–5 with links), then Work Experience. Include GPA only if 3.5+. Include relevant coursework only if 4–6 directly-relevant courses can be named (skip generic intro courses). Generate Project bullets with scope and tech-stack depth as substitutes for missing outcome metrics. Never include "Objective", high-school jobs after sophomore year, or generic computer-literacy skills.
chunks.body:
# Intern / Student Resume Conventions

Intern resumes are evaluated on potential, not track record. They demonstrate (a) technical fundamentals (coursework + projects), (b) execution evidence (shipped projects, hackathons, OSS), (c) conscientiousness signals (GPA if strong, awards). MIT CAPD: include class projects, competitions, and personal projects alongside formal work experience.

**Length:** strictly one page.

**Section order:**
1. Contact info.
2. Education (top — strongest credential at this stage).
3. Skills.
4. Projects (3–5 strongest, with links).
5. Work experience (internships, part-time, research assistantships).
6. Awards / honors (if relevant).

**Education specifics:**
- Degree, university, expected graduation (`Expected May 2026`).
- GPA only if 3.5+ on 4.0 scale (3.7+ for FAANG-tier). Include local-scale equivalent for non-US.
- Relevant coursework: 4–6 directly-relevant courses max. Skip generic intros.
- Honors: Dean's List (with semester count), scholarships.

**Projects specifics:**
- 3–5 projects with name, tech stack, 1–3 bullets, GitHub or live link.
- Prefer shipped projects (deployed, in app store, 10+ GitHub stars) over coursework.
- Hackathon projects fine if they shipped.

**Work experience without internships:**
- Part-time jobs (TA, tutor, retail) if they show responsibility or work ethic.
- Research assistantships count as work, not coursework.
- Drop high-school jobs after sophomore year.

**What NOT to include:**
- "Objective: To gain valuable experience..." — outdated.
- Generic computer-literacy skills (MS Office) — assumed.
- Photo, marital status, religion (for international apps). MENA differs — see `mena_context/`.

**Bullet pattern for intern projects:**
CAR / X-Y-Z still apply. With limited metrics, substitute:
- **Scope:** lines of code, files, modules, features.
- **Tech stack depth:** signals production-tier choices, not tutorial copy.
- **Process signals:** "wrote 28 pytest cases", "set up GitHub Actions CI", "deployed on Railway".
- **Recognition:** stars, hackathon placement, course awards.

**Strong intern bullet examples:**

- "Built a CLI tool converting Markdown to Anki flashcards (Python + Click); 1.2K LOC, 24 GitHub stars, used by 40+ in r/medicalschool."
- "Co-led a 4-person team in the university hackathon (24h); shipped a real-time campus-shuttle tracker (React Native + Firebase + GTFS); placed 2nd of 32."
- "Contributed 6 PRs to Django REST Framework docs; 4 merged including a throttle-class clarification referenced in the project newsletter."

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

For intern / student resumes, generate one-page output with Education at the top, followed by Skills, then Projects (3–5 with links), then Work Experience. Include GPA only if 3.5+. Include relevant coursework only if 4–6 directly-relevant courses can be named (skip generic intro courses). Generate Project bullets with scope and tech-stack depth as substitutes for missing outcome metrics. Never include "Objective", high-school jobs after sophomore year, or generic computer-literacy skills.

---
sources:
  - https://capd.mit.edu/resources/resumes/  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9sum%C3%A9  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.jobscan.co/blog/20-ats-friendly-resume-templates/  (accessed 2026-05-12)

chunks.title: Junior (0–2 years experience) Resume Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: For junior resumes, generate one-page output with 4–5 work-experience bullets per role, mixing feature-build, refactor, investigation, and process-improvement patterns. Use Contributed to / Owned (scoped piece) / Implemented rather than Led / Spearheaded / Architected. Include at least one metric per bullet; for bullets without numerical metrics, use scope (modules, PRs reviewed, hires onboarded, runbooks authored). Keep Projects section if the candidate has strong personal-project material.
chunks.body:
# Junior (0–2 years experience) Resume Conventions

A junior candidate has 0–2 years post-graduation experience — usually one full-time job, possibly with 1–2 internships. The resume sits between intern and mid-level. It demonstrates (a) shipping real code in a production team, (b) skill set matured beyond university projects, (c) clear progression direction.

**Length:** strictly one page.

**Section order:**
1. Contact info.
2. Professional summary (2–3 sentences).
3. Skills (grouped).
4. Work experience (1–2 roles, 4–5 bullets each).
5. Projects (backfills limited work history).
6. Education.
7. Optional: Certifications, Awards.

**Professional summary:** role + duration + one concrete proof point.

- BAD: "Recent CS graduate passionate about leveraging cutting-edge technology."
- GOOD: "Backend engineer, 14 months at FintechCo on the payments-reconciliation team; shipped v2 reconciliation that brought match-rate from 91% to 99.4%."

**Bullet patterns:**
- 4–5 per role, mixing feature-build with one or two process/learning bullets.
- Quantification expected, at smaller scale than mid-level.
- Avoid inflated leadership verbs. Use Contributed to / Helped ship / Owned (scoped piece) rather than Led / Spearheaded.
- Mentioning pair-programming or mentorship received is fine.

**Strong junior bullet examples:**

- "Owned end-to-end build of the customer-data export feature (FastAPI + Celery + S3 + email); shipped in 4 weeks, used by 220 customers in month 1."
- "Refactored 12 legacy Django views into the new FastAPI service in Q3; test coverage rose from 38% to 81% across migrated endpoints."
- "Contributed 8 of 22 modules in the v2 admin dashboard rewrite (React + TS); notification-settings module shipped 1 sprint ahead of estimate."
- "Investigated 4 recurring p95 spikes; root-caused 3 to connection-pool exhaustion; added monitoring now standard in the service-template."
- "Wrote the team's onboarding-doc playbook; new hires' time-to-first-PR dropped from ~10 days to 4 days."

**Junior anti-patterns:**

- Padding 2 years into 4 dense bullets with inflated language. Recruiters can tell.
- Claiming "Led" or "Architected" for IC scope. "Implemented JWT refresh-token logic" honest; "Architected a microservices auth system" at 14 months inflated.
- No metrics. Find numbers even at small scale.
- Hiding graduation year — recruiters notice the gap.

**Projects section.** Strong personal projects strengthen junior resumes. Keep 3–5 strongest from intern resume, focused on technical decisions.

**"First job" anchor.** Most-recent role bullets should clear the "can ship code in a team" bar — one on code review, one on shipping to prod, one on user benefit.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

For junior resumes, generate one-page output with 4–5 work-experience bullets per role, mixing feature-build, refactor, investigation, and process-improvement patterns. Use Contributed to / Owned (scoped piece) / Implemented rather than Led / Spearheaded / Architected. Include at least one metric per bullet; for bullets without numerical metrics, use scope (modules, PRs reviewed, hires onboarded, runbooks authored). Keep Projects section if the candidate has strong personal-project material.

---
sources:
  - https://capd.mit.edu/resources/resumes/  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.indeed.com/career-advice/resumes-cover-letters/software-engineer-resume  (accessed 2026-05-12)
  - https://www.indeed.com/career-advice/resume-mistakes-to-avoid/  (accessed 2026-05-12)

chunks.title: Mid-Level (3–7 years experience) Resume Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: For mid-level resumes, generate 3–5 bullets per recent role and 2–3 for older roles; total 1–2 pages. Mix feature-build, architecture, performance/reliability, and influence/mentorship bullet types within each role. Drop university coursework and GPA. Generate the Professional Summary with role + years + specialization + 1–2 concrete proof points (not "experienced engineer"). Generate at least one bullet per recent role demonstrating technical influence beyond the candidate's own code (mentorship, design-doc authorship, cross-team RFC).
chunks.body:
# Mid-Level (3–7 years experience) Resume Conventions

A mid-level candidate has 3–7 years of experience — typically 2–3 jobs. The resume demonstrates (a) independent end-to-end feature ownership, (b) deepening specialization or breadth-with-depth, (c) influence on team practices, mentorship, or architecture.

**Length:** one page standard; two acceptable for technical roles with deep stack + projects.

**Section order:**
1. Contact info.
2. Professional summary (2–3 sentences: role + years + 1–2 proof points).
3. Skills.
4. Work experience (3–5 bullets per recent role, 2–3 for older).
5. Education (degree, university, year; coursework dropped).
6. Optional: Projects (if notable OSS or side-project work), Certifications.

**Professional summary:** role + total years + specialization + 1–2 proof points.

- BAD: "Experienced software engineer with a passion for building scalable solutions."
- GOOD: "Backend engineer, 5 years; specialized in payment systems on Postgres + Kafka. Most recently led the migration to event-sourced billing, dropping reconciliation errors from 3% to under 0.1% for $12M/yr GMV."

**Bullet patterns:**
- 3–5 per recent role, 2–3 for older.
- Mix feature-build, architecture, performance, and influence/mentorship.
- Quantify at production / business-metric scale.
- Leadership verbs (Led, Owned, Designed) for genuinely owned projects.
- One bullet per recent role on influence beyond own code (design docs, mentorship, RFC).

**Strong mid-level bullet examples:**

- "Owned design + implementation of payment-retry (Postgres advisory locks + idempotency keys); duplicate charges fell from 0.4% to 0.01%; system processes 14K retries/day."
- "Led REST-to-gRPC migration across 8 services in Q3; median inter-service latency fell from 92ms to 38ms; deprecated 3,400 lines of REST boilerplate."
- "Authored the API design guide (consumed in every new-service review since Mar 2025); mentored 2 mid-level engineers through their first cross-team RFCs."
- "Reduced monthly AWS spend from $48K to $34K over 4 months by right-sizing 22 RDS instances and moving cold S3 data to Glacier."
- "Fixed the search-staleness bug on the backlog for 8 months; root cause was an Elasticsearch index-alias rotation race; fix has held 11 months."

**Mid-level anti-patterns:**

- Still listing coursework / GPA. Drop unless GPA is exceptional and the role rewards academic credentials.
- "Senior-style" leadership claims without scope. "Led a project" is more honest than "Led a team of N" without people-management.
- Listing every project from earlier career. The intern hackathon shouldn't still be on the resume at year 5.
- Identical-shape bullets across all roles.

**Promotion-readiness signal.** Mid-level candidates applying for senior should signal readiness through (a) 1–2 senior-shaped bullets (architecture, mentorship, cross-team influence), and (b) a Summary framing depth not years.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

For mid-level resumes, generate 3–5 bullets per recent role and 2–3 for older roles; total 1–2 pages. Mix feature-build, architecture, performance/reliability, and influence/mentorship bullet types within each role. Drop university coursework and GPA. Generate the Professional Summary with role + years + specialization + 1–2 concrete proof points (not "experienced engineer"). Generate at least one bullet per recent role demonstrating technical influence beyond the candidate's own code (mentorship, design-doc authorship, cross-team RFC).

---

chunks.title: Senior+ (8+ years experience) Resume Conventions
chunks.concrete_rule: For senior+ resumes, generate 4–6 bullets per recent role with at least one architecture bullet and one influence bullet per role. Compress roles older than 12 years to single-line summaries. Use Led / Designed / Owned / Drove / Mentored / Hired / Decided more often than Built / Implemented (target: under 30% feature-build verbs). Generate the Professional Summary as 3–4 sentences naming distinctive specialization + scope + 1–2 proof points. Drop university coursework, GPA, and most lower-context skills from the Skills section.
chunks.body:
# Senior+ (8+ years experience) Resume Conventions

A senior+ engineer has 8+ years experience and multiple jobs where individual feature work is no longer the headline. The resume demonstrates (a) sustained impact at scope, (b) influence beyond direct deliverables, (c) judgment under uncertainty.

**Length:** two pages standard; three for principal+ with deep OSS.

**Section order:** Contact, Summary (3–4 sentences), Skills (grouped, lighter), Work experience (4–6 bullets per recent role, 2–3 for older, single-line for >12-year roles), Selected projects / OSS / publications (only if notable), Education (one line).

**Professional summary:** 3–4 sentences. Role + years + distinctive expertise + 1–2 proof points.

- BAD: "Highly accomplished senior software engineer with extensive experience."
- GOOD: "Backend engineer, 12 years; deep specialization in payment systems. Most recently led the platform-wide migration to event-sourced billing across 4 product lines and $40M annual GMV; previously owned core payment-routing through 8x traffic growth at a Series-C fintech."

**Bullet patterns:**
- 4–6 bullets per recent role demonstrating scope (multi-team, multi-quarter), architecture (trade-offs), influence (mentorship, hiring).
- Quantification shifts toward business outcomes (revenue, customer impact, org metrics).
- Each recent role needs at least one architecture bullet and one influence bullet.

**Strong senior bullet examples:**

- "Designed and led the platform-wide migration from monolithic billing to event-sourced architecture (CQRS over Kafka, Postgres snapshot store, gRPC query API); 14-month timeline, 4 squads, zero data-loss incidents during cutover."
- "Owned technical strategy for the Reliability working group across 6 teams; established the SLO framework; platform P0 incidents fell from 14 in 2023 to 4 in 2024."
- "Hired and onboarded 6 backend engineers in 2024; designed the interview rubric; first-year retention 6/6 vs. org baseline of 4/6."
- "Authored 8 RFCs in 2024 (5 approved); the approved set included Postgres logical replication, now standard across 22 services."
- "Mentored 4 mid-level engineers to senior; 3 promoted within 14 months; mentorship rubric I drafted is used by eng-management across the org."

**Senior-level anti-patterns:**

- Leading with feature-build verbs (Built, Implemented). Use Led / Designed / Owned / Drove / Mentored / Hired / Decided. Under 30% feature-build.
- Listing every technology. What differentiates is judgment about *when* to use which.
- No hiring, mentorship, or process bullets. Senior IC and management both need one.
- Older-than-12-year roles with full bullet detail. Compress to single lines.
- Self-promotion without trade-off acknowledgement reads as a red flag.

**Staff / principal.** Patterns shift toward strategy: vision docs, org-design proposals, conference talks, OSS maintainership.

## Concrete rule for SmartCV

For senior+ resumes, generate 4–6 bullets per recent role with at least one architecture bullet and one influence bullet per role. Compress roles older than 12 years to single-line summaries. Use Led / Designed / Owned / Drove / Mentored / Hired / Decided more often than Built / Implemented (target: under 30% feature-build verbs). Generate the Professional Summary as 3–4 sentences naming distinctive specialization + scope + 1–2 proof points. Drop university coursework, GPA, and most lower-context skills from the Skills section.

---
